comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: If you have a statistically improbable run of bad luck, does that mean you are jinxed?  If so, how can you break the jinx?  I can do natural magic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The first thing to figure out is whether it's actually bad luck. Very often what people think of as "bad luck" is the precise consequences of their own misguided attitudes and actions. Tolerably often, too, collective factors are involved -- for example, people who have trouble getting a job may be applying to an overcrowded field when business is contracting. Roughly 90% of the time, when people approach me saying that they're jinxed, one or both of those is involved.

It's fairly easy to figure out if there are collective factors involved -- how many other people do you know of who are getting the same results? It's much harder to catch yourself setting yourself up to fail. A serious talk with a friend you can trust to tell you the truth can help, and so can journaling, but one way or another, it's usually a slow process.

If it is a genuine jinx, there's actually quite a simple way to dodge it: change what you do and how you do it. Abandon your existing habits and pick up new habits. Find a job in an unfamiliar field, take up a hobby you never thought of doing, start associating with people you'd never have considered socializing with. A jinx is a rut of bad luck; break yourself out of your other ruts and you'll pop yourself out of that one, too.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you!  This sounds reasonable and practical.

replies.content: The first thing to do is work with all the spheres in order, first from the bottom up, and then from the top down. That establishes a framework of balance. Then you can choose one sphere and do a series of workings with it. What kind of workings? Depends on what kind you like best. comments.title: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
comments.content:
Good Evening,

For any who mark it, I hope you have a lovely winter solstice this week, and for anyone who observes Christmas, I hope that goes well for you too.

To Share: Plugging away at Seed of Yggdrasill here: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_the_seed_of_yggdrasill_10_the_wise_man_s_path

To Ask: Some folks I've been reading lately make the argument that pretty much all of what we take for granted as modern morality derives from Christianity, and that other religious and philosophical traditions lacked many of the things most folks today would consider core parts of morality, like forgiveness, the intrinsic dignity of mankind, and so forth. My intuition is that this is not right, or at least "right in a misleading way" at most, but I don't know what sources to go to for good counter-arguments. Any recommendations from JMG or the commentariat?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
replies.content:
Pagan morality differs from Christian morality, no question. What we take for granted as modern morality, though -- insofar as anything can be taken for granted these days! -- is a free mix of Christian, Jewish, classical Greek, and traditional northern and western European morality, and the Christian contribution is by no means the largest or the most essential. Are forgiveness and the supposed intrinsic dignity of mankind really the most important moral principles? Only to those who are trying to make a polemic point.

I recommend Alasdair McIntyre's fine book After Virtue as a guide to virtue ethics, grounded largely on classical Greek morality. Of course Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics and Eudemian Ethics are always worthwhile.

replies.title: Re: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
replies.content:
Thank you! And fair enough. After Virtue has been on my list a long time, and it looks like this upcoming year is the time to tackle it (for the first time, anyway).

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
replies.content:
I have been thinking about this stuff a lot lately (as a foreigner who has recently moved to America) and yeah I think its a big mix of influences that plays out in different ways. Protestantism seems core to America's individualist expression particularly, but the design of the state has much more greek/roman ideas (all the greek/roman larping buildings give a solid hint of that).

I think the founding fathers had virtue in mind but virtue isn't greatly expressed here in current day. Protestantism unlocked the individual willing and the secularization of that, including the loosening of guilt conditioning in the 60s has created much more selfishness and narcissism today (because virtue conditioning was never implemented here that would preclude the dominance of the excess self).

replies.title: Re: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
replies.content: Thank you for sharing your perspective. Having grown up right in the middle of it, it's helpful to hear from folks who don't take the same things for granted.

replies.title: Re: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
replies.content: Another book recommendation is "A secular age" by Charles Taylor. Might club well with "After Virtue".

replies.title: Re: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
replies.content: Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
replies.content:
Greetings Jeff!

Morality has been a pondering point for me of late (particularly as it relates to raising my children.)

Reading the arguments you are facing, the most pertinent response that comes to mind for me is that morality is not a simple thing, nor even a complicated thing, it is alive. So, sure, some cultures did not value forgiveness in their cultural teachings, and perhaps that was reflected in their daily life, and perhaps not. If we fast forward a thousand years and read moral teachings from this age, how well would they represent what humanity is actually doing? ...

There were likely persons, in all times and amongst cultures, who valued forgiveness. There were likely cultures that valued forgiveness for the village and atonement or revenge for those outside it. Of course you could argue that exists here and now as well. There were likely individuals in warrior cultures who exhibited Christian morality, and individuals in monastic meditative cultures who were quite Machiavellian.

Anyway, no matter the "proof" anyone can show of what certain cultures held to be moral, the reality likely differed; and differed person to person.

Thanks for the questions and for your writing.

replies.title: Re: Seed of Yggdrasill 10 and Question about Non-Christian Morality
replies.content:
Huh, that's a rather interesting way to think about it, and sounds like it bears further pondering (from me, I mean - you can ponder or not as you like!)

The great gift of embracing gnosis and validity as the cores of a religious path over doxos and authenticity is that there's lots of room to encounter and explore the living complexities of the spiritual life. The great price is that you have to wrestle with those complexities, because there're are no ready-made answers. Turns out that's a pretty big can of worms to open up :)

Jeff

comments.title: Christian magic books
comments.content: Hi John! I'm new to the whole magic and occult scene. Are there any good books on Christian magic? Or is that a bit of an oxymoron? Haha!

replies.title: Re: Christian magic books
replies.content:
Not an oxymoron at all; well over half of the great mages and occultists of the last fifteen hundred years of Western history have been Christians, some of them unusually devout. The book I recommend to Christians interested in occult study is Experience of the Inner Worlds by Gareth Knight. Knight, who died not long ago, was an Anglican Christian, very serious about his faith, and a first-rate operative magician.

There are plenty of other books on Christian magic but that's the one to start with.

replies.title: Re: Christian magic books
replies.content: If I may be so bold, once you've finished Knight's excellent book, there are two books I'd recommend: "Ritual Magic for Conservative Christians" and/or "The Magic of Catholicism: Real Magic for Devout Catholics," both by Agostino Taumaturgo.

comments.title: Tracks in space
comments.content:
I have recently come to see, that attempts to do various things in a novel or tweaked way at work or in life is extremely difficult. It always falls into the old familiar tracks. Even when all involved agree that the old way of doing those things is not viable and nobody wants to do things that way. But when the shovel hits the gravel, figuratively, it is the same old thing. And it looks like it is a pretty widespread phenomenon, actually. The old way is not working and it looks like the new is just not coming out.
So my question is how to get ones' attention off from the battle with the old? Intuition recommends to disengage and be a hermit, but that gets boring for someone used to and interested in "doing" things. Ha - now that I wrote this, I notice the doing-pressure I cling to. It is hard to just be, especially this time of the year, this point in our civilization. Just wanting so much to "help" the new light to come - as if it were at all dependent on me.
Gamboge Cantankerous Oyster

replies.title: Re: Tracks in space
replies.content: The best approach is focus on changing your own habits in your own life. Don't try to change all of them at once -- just choose one, change it, and stick to the change. Then change another. That puts down tracks in space in the direction of loosening the grip of habit, and bit by bit, that affects the world.

comments.title: Hypothesis redux
comments.content:
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/145097.html?thread=18158793#cmt18158793

Here's a comment from Sept 2021, in response to (one of) our host's infamous hypotheses. The original poster had been doing divinations concerning the subsequent years. 2022 and '23 were deemed "unstable", and '24 would display "very, very dark energies, but different from the current ones". From '25 onward, much better.
It was actually long after being posted that I read this, but I found it consistent with what I personally felt.
From Spring '21 to Fall '22, I have indeed been feeling like the world had become really inhospitable, at times I thought I would go under... Obviously I willy-nilly rode the wave and am still here but it's been scary.
2023, although indeed unstable on the world stage (geopolitical, etc.), has felt like the clouds were breaking, the air has been "lighter". As it draws to a close though, I think I have seen some inklings of the heaviness again, possibly confiming the OP's musings. If said OP recognizes him/herself, he/she is invited to chime in ;-), as is anybody who wants to, of course.

Since the darkness of '21 and '24 ought not to be the same, what are we looking at ? The rational mind says warfare on a larger scale than it already is, or economic difficulties compounding and impacting larger swathes of populations. Beyond rationality, one can imagine a resurgence of humans displaying anti-human behavior, a sort of non-human hatred that's "in the air", picked up by unsuspecting folks and projected to others in their vicinity. Appetizing prospect.

Circling back to the hypothesis, could it be that the 7-year "deal", or those "deals" in general, end with a bang rather than a whimper ? Instead of gradually fading, that they would generate a metaphorical supernova event ? That would give context to the deleterious effluvia I believe I've been gettin' anew. The early innings of the last patch of rapids, if you will.

Not the jolliest of posts, I'm afraid. It does contain a glimmer of hope, so there's that. All the best to everyone.

~Thibault

replies.title: Re: Hypothesis redux
replies.content:
Good question. Right now we're in the window where my original hypothesis will be proved or disproved; if in fact a lot of people who were involved in the "Magic Resistance" die over the months immediately ahead, the hypothesis is going to look very likely, while if they do not, the hypothesis is disproved. As I noted at the time, it was a tentative and speculative hypothesis, and we'll just have to see.

As for the darkness of 2024, there are several factors already in motion that could drive that quite readily. The Western economies are in deep trouble, not least because their attempt to punish Russia with sanctions blew back in their own faces so spectacularly; the war in Ukraine is going Russia's way now, with Russian troops advancing steadily on four different fronts; an extraordinarily large number of people in the US and other highly vaccinated countries are sick just now, and don't seem to be getting better; and the list goes on. An economic crisis combined with the worldwide political blowback from a NATO defeat in Ukraine and a high death rate domestically could produce a very bleak year!

comments.title: The gods behind the gods
comments.content:
I came across a clip of a discussion between Jonathan Pageau and Jordan Peterson recently where they were discussing AI.

I usually scroll past that sort of thing with a weary eye roll. But the clip's title did the clickbait trick so I listened.

They were discussing AI in the context of being a new God. Pageau said something that really struck me. He referred to AI as hyper divination. I'm not altogether interested in this sort of thing but I do try to keep tabs on current fads and obsessions and this intrigued me given our societies current death drive.

What sort of results could be expected from using something so tied up with our Faustian bargain with deeply buried ancient sunlight? Skynet is on all our minds of course but could something so covered in ick be turned to good?

I have recently been pondering on the deeper meaning of the machine in our psyches. After the enlightenment when we were busily chasing enchantment out of every corner of our lives the machine was merely a labor saver, there only to ease our difficulty.

But it quickly became an archetype and it wasn't long before we began to see our imperfections reflected in the machine's glossy surface.

Throughout the 19th and into the early 20th century we attempted to make man behave like a machine. The result was big piles of bodies.

Seeing that failure we inverted the premise and decided to make machines more human and so we busied ourselves with robots and automation for a while. That was fantasy of course because robots are creepy and unnerving and automation resulted in nothing more than the cheapening of our humanity.

The man/machine equivalency is of course a category error. They could never be combined outside of procrustean butchery which is as always a very real possibility still.

With the advent of the environmental movement though we're done with humanity. We see ourselves as a plague and a curse on the planet. We would all be better off gone (so say the delightfully dimwitted protesters).

So it's no surprise that now the machine must transcend humanity and become a God. I think there is a deep desire for that God to be a vengeful one.

But what if something beyond both man and machine decides to take this unprecedented opportunity to speak with such literal directness to an audience so primed with self loathing?

Cheery thoughts for this blustery grey Monday!

replies.title: Re: The gods behind the gods
replies.content:
Re "big piles of bodies": To be fair, while the French Revolution might I guess have been close to having Enlightenment ideas about mechanicality and applying some of them to mankind (especially "meaningless"-seeming collective things like politics and culture), once we got to the 20th century, major other things were going on. German idealist philosophy had gotten Western-educated elites everywhere into an invisible intellectual frame that pushed to make collectivities look like the highest material manifestation of what was real and valuable about the world, independent individuals look like recalicitrant meaningless raw matter (even if they happened to not be mechanical) which must only be made to yield, blood-drenched wartime slaughter look like a laudable humanity-elevating form of high-level philosophical deliberation pursued by a higher mind, and individual rights look like a backward myth. And the cultures deriving from German idealist philosophy were, after a certain point, often quite hostile to the cultural residues of Enlightenment philosophy. Goebbels's technology-valorizing "steely romanticism" had to be invented, and even then, he rejected the idea of mere mechanicality:

"[...] we face the danger of modern technology to dehumanize people. National socialism has never opposed the technology. Far from being opposed to the technology, one of the main tasks of national socialism have been to accept it consciously, to ensoul it, to control it, to put it into the service of the people and to bring it into conformity with the cultural level of the people. National socialist public expressions were used to refer to the steely romanticism of our age. We live in both a romantic and steel-like age. This age has not lost its spiritual depth. We have discovered a new romanticism, through the implications of modern inventions and new technologies. As national socialists, we know exactly how to see the soulless frame of technology and how to fill it by getting it up to date."

It might seem strange to see a Nazi going on about how there is a danger of "dehumanizing people", but in Goebbels's conception, "humanization" has to do with a symbolic-meaningfulness investment of subsumption in the mythic identity and fate-duty of one's people, not with a symbolic-meaningfulness investment of rights or dignity.

It's possible that a correct analysis would, rather than speaking about thinking of things as machines, recognize this as a metonym for falsely judging some things to have none of the meaningfulness and other things to have all the meaningfulness, whether the things be individuals, collectives, biological organisms, or mechanical processes.

replies.title: Re: The gods behind the gods
replies.content:
That's been a theme in science fiction for most of a century now: having failed to transcend the human condition, supposedly we can create something that will do it for us. The vengeful quality is inevitable, since part of the drive to create that thing is the desire to punish humanity for not living up to its own delusions of grandeur. In reality, of course, these supposed "AIs" are actually just brute-force models that imitate the more mechanical aspects of human activity; it's indicative that there have been so many warnings recently that if the "AIs" are allowed to base their modeling on other machine-generated content, they quickly go spinning out into babbling incoherence.

My working guess is that the powers beyond humanity are watching this whole thing with mild amusement, and will respond to it in their usual way, by allowing the whole ramshackle fantasy to come crashing down around its true believers.

replies.title: Re: The gods behind the gods
replies.content:
Well... I can see why spurious data from confabulated worlds would make a problem for just-recently-trailing-edge AI architectures. But I can also see how I would start to make a machine better able to orient to the range of possible worlds and subjective placements in those worlds, so that the AI would be able to on some level apprehend the distinctions between, and flexibly make task-directed decisions about, the option of trying to speak from within the central original world where most of its data is from, with its own state of knowledge, versus the options of trying to speak from within various self-confabulated or other-confabulated worlds, with imagined states of confabulated knowledge. And I can see that, indeed, other people have now started to do the main first thing I would do there, even if only in a rudimentary way. And I can see how the path leading from there does naturally come to involve substantially decreasing the amount of brute force and increasing the amount of insight. I don't know how long it would take, but it would be very surprising if producing this basic ability took more than eleven years unhindered.

Part of the basis for our beliefs is the idea that AI is something that people will almost certainly keep improving over time. This fact you cite, that other people felt the need to publish warnings about an inadequacy of a recent-past architecture, because they knew that still other people might have found that inadequacy counterintuitive given that architecture's brute-forced seeming abilities, isn't strong evidence toward the question of whether there is something wrong with our thought process behind our estimates about eventual better future architectures. Especially in that we, ourselves, wouldn't have found that inadequacy counterintuitive, and so we wouldn't have needed the warning, even if the others did. It could only be very abstract evidence about some other shared propensity for having things wrong with our thinking.

On 3), if I might, a handful of suggestions:

a. John Cleese of Monty Python fame has a lovely little book called Creativity: a Short and Cheerful Guide which very much lives up to its title. Besides some how-to tips, it also tells Cleese's story of going from "not a creative person" to, you know, John Cleese.

b. The War of Art by Steven Pressfield also talks about the kinds of things that hold us back from pursuing creative endeavors, and acknowledges the wide range of things that can count as "art" (building a business, writing a book, running a household, etc). Also very short.

c. Here's a technique you can start with: when you want to come up with an idea, first come up with a lot of ideas, with zero concern for whether they're good or would work or are even entirely on topic. Set yourself an arbitrary number or an amount of time to spend on it (for example, "I'll come up with 15 ideas for a creative project" or "I'll spend 10 minutes coming up with as many project ideas as I can". Once you've come up with a lot of ideas, now you worry about which ones are good, which ones could be combined, or if there's an even better idea that comes to you while you're evaluating. To sum up: first, generate, then evaluate.

Anyhow, hope some of this helps, and good luck!
Jeff

Maybe not a change in eye color or the regrowth of a limb, but something like glamour (such as the regrowth of hair or the thickening of a beard in men) or the reduction of some skin imperfection?

Especially since, according to the grimuars, we know that there are demons capable of influencing people's physical bodies (grimorum Verum)

replies.title: Re: Magic and body
replies.content: Of course. The mind influences the body, just as the body influences the mind. That said, if you're caught up in the male equivalent of the mental state of those teenaged girls who watched Charmed and deluged online occultists begging for spells to turn their hair blonde, you're likely to be about as successful as they were! Why not work instead to stop putting so much emotional energy into your hairline or a skin problem? That's going to have much more success. (And since other people are much more strongly influenced by your personality and how you project your presence than they are by physical factors, even when they don't realize this, it's also a more effective approach.)

replies.title: Re: Magic and body

replies.title: Re: Magic and body

replies.title: Re: Magic and body

Modern psychology is hamstrung by the materialist worldview of Western science, and it's also been around for so short a time that it's hardly to the toddling stage yet. Today's magical traditions can trace their history back more than five thousand years; we've learned quite a few things of which psychologists have no clue.

comments.title: Question on Planar Dynamics
comments.content:
Greetings and glad Yule / Solstice / Christmas etc to all!

I've been thinking about what you write about the progress of souls through the planes, in your "Occult Philosophy Workbook". The sequential progress of a soul through the material, etheric, and astral planes seems quite self-evident. I have some questions, though, when it comes to the apparent necessity to pass through the mental plane before reaching the spiritual.

I can think of examples of well-known spiritual people who, it seems to me, were well into the spiritual realm without, apparently, much strength or experience in the mental realm. Two examples are Brother Lawrence, and Julian of Norwich, in the Christian contemplative tradition.

I contrast them with two other examples from the same tradition who clearly seem to have 'mastered' much of the mental realm on their way to the spiritual realm, namely the polymath Hildegard of Bingen, and Teresa of Avila. These 4 people were all devout, experienced, and far along on their spiritual paths, and shared a number of practices in common. Yet, 2 of them I would say are true exemplars of people well along in the mental plane, while the other 2 don't show much sign of that. I suspect we might find many more Brother Lawrence / Dame Julian examples in many faith traditions around the world, if we knew where to look.

To me, it seems that the path between the upper astral plane and the spiritual plane can in some instances be a direct one, without a lot of development in the mental plane. Almost as though some people can create a worm-hole or tunnel through the mental plane and move directly into the spiritual. Or as though there is some fork in the road, where one can (however indirectly or unconsciously) choose either to go through the mental plane to get to the spiritual, or go direct from the upper astral into the spiritual plane. It seems to me that there is a good deal of similarity or continuity between upper astral and spiritual planes.

I'd much appreciate reading your thoughts on this, and add my thanks to everyone else's for your provision of this fine discussion space.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Question on Planar Dynamics
replies.content: I think a lot of the issue here is a matter of terminology. The mental plane is not the plane of intellectual activities -- the part of it that we can perceive at this stage of our evolution is a realm of meaning, purpose, and value, which can be expressed in intellectual forms but also in other forms. It's been a while since I last read either Julian of Norwich or Brother Lawrence, but my recollection is that both of them -- while not intellectual -- had very keen and well-developed senses of meaning, purpose, and value. So their development on the mental plane didn't happen to engage with the expressions we call "intellectual," at least in the incarnation we know about.

replies.title: Re: Question on Planar Dynamics
replies.content:
Thank you--that's a helpful perspective; I can definitely see that. Back to work on "The Occult Philosophy Workbook," which I love!

Winifred

replies.title: Re: Question on Planar Dynamics
replies.content: “Meaning, purpose, and value” are culturally determined and shared-that is usually part of a stock definition of “culture”. So that’s why we have to break away from our culture at the mental sheath development stage?-Berserker

replies.title: Re: Question on Planar Dynamics
replies.content: They're culturally determined until you reach the point of being able to perceive them for yourself. Yes, that's why you have to be able to do that to complete the evolution of the mental body. That doesn't necessarily mean you reject those of your culture -- but you perceive them directly and act accordingly, rather than simply following what you've been taught.

replies.title: Re: Question on Planar Dynamics
replies.content: That further explains the bit about “the way of the lonely ones”….. Thanks!

replies.content:

Maybe I do not understand consciousness. If I sit on a park bench hoping someone sits down and talks to me or the hot dog vendor shows up soon, I do not think I am conscious.

If I sit there and think about how to minimize suffering throughout the world and have an understanding of myself and how I interact with the 250 people I know, I am conscious.

How any of this relates to a nonhuman being? I do not have a clue.

I remember being about 3 years old and thinking, "Hey, these people I see walking around? The ones I talk to? Me, I am one of those.

replies.title: Re: Fundamental
replies.content:
No, you've gotten trapped in ideological rhetoric. "Conscious" in the mouths of political activists is shorthand for "conscious of the problem we want you to obsess about."

This is the more basic sense. Let's say some sinister Druid sneaks up behind you and knocks you unconscious with a whack from his oaken staff. You're unconscious -- you don't see anything, hear anything, think anything, feel anything. Then, slowly, you surface to find that you've been set down on a convenient park bench by a friendly hot dog vendor; you're conscious again -- meaning you see things, hear things, etc. That's what we're discussing.

replies.title: Re: 5 Rites, Kundalini, etc

replies.title: Necromancy
replies.content:
Heathen seidhr and similar forms of magic work with the ancestors and the dead. I'd recommend Cat Heath's website, Seo Helrune, her occasional excellent webinars, and her book "Elves, Witches & Gods: Spinning Old Heathen Magic in the Modern Day." The book offers thorough grounding for this kind of work, and the website talks about fascinating experiments she's doing, trying to reconstruct old patterns of working with earth-grids, mound-sitting, meditating at archaeological sites, and so forth to contact the ancestors. She's been working at this for a couple of decades at least, primarily in England but now expanding to the east coast of the US. She has some pretty amazing insights, and they are based on deep research and deep experience, both.

Diana Paxson's book "The Way of the Oracle: Recovering the Practices of the Past to Find Answers for Today," discusses a lot of interesting practices that bear relationships to necromancy. Diana's book "Trance-Portation" offers excellent training in the techniques needed for this kind of work.

I hope it goes without saying that this is not a path to undertake lightly! It's truly essential to proceed slowly and cautiously, learn from others with experience, and support your own wellbeing with a hefty dose of common sense, self-discipline, and self-care. Josephine McCarthy's books on magical healing and related topics are useful resources here, and Cat offers practical guidelines as well.

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: God's Time
comments.content:
JMG,

I've been thinking about something you said: that Gods experience all time at once.

Does this lead to some kind of determinism? It would seem that if you pray for something (say a new job) that God would already know whether you would get the job or not. God knew you would pray to them before you did. Knowing the past and the future... well, it would seem a rather dreary existence.

Or perhaps the future is not ordained. The future is constantly changing a million times a second with all the small decisions being made. Now THAT would be pretty cool. Like watching the flames of a roaring fire or the northern lights,... or something.

I'm interested in your thoughts.

Thanks,
-Slink

replies.title: Re: God's Time
replies.content:
That's a common misunderstanding, caused by the inability to think through the implications of being outside of time. To an eternal being, your free will doesn't interfere with their ability to know what you do at a future time, because for them it's not a future time. If all time is now, they can see you make the decision when you get around to making it, and also all the other decisions you make freely at every other moment when you make them.

As for prayer, the point of prayer isn't to influence the gods. The point of prayer is its effect on your consciousness. That's one of the reasons that "gimmee-gimmee-gimmee!" prayer is a waste of time; the gods are not vending machines. Just talk to them...

replies.title: Re: God's Time
replies.content: Is this the other half (or perhaps the other eighth) of the meaning behind "magic is the art of changing consciousness at will?"  We pray to change our consciousness at the Gods' will.

replies.title: Re: God's Time
replies.content: That's certainly another part of it.

replies.title: Re: God's Time
replies.content:
I'm sorry, maybe I phrased it wrong.

If they know what I will do at a future time, doesn't this mean that the future is already known to them? Like they're watching a movie? (I realize watching a movie takes time, which is not relevant per se). Again, knowing everything that will happen seems,... boring.

I apologize for my confusion.

-Slink

replies.title: Re: God's Time
replies.content:
No, it's not that you've phrased it wrong. It's that you're misunderstanding what "outside of time" means. For eternal beings, the future -- all the future -- is right now. So is all the past. Nothing "will happen" for them and nothing "has happened" for them. It's all happening right now in the infinite present moment of eternity.

Thus it's all fresh and new for them, far more so than it can be for us, because we inevitably compare the present to the past -- but for them, there is no past; everything is right now. Your entire life is a single action unfolding in one eternal moment. So is the life of the entire universe. So is the life of the gods.

I know, it's hard to get a human mind around this. We're so used to living in time!

comments.title: A Juvenile Culture
comments.content:
I recently had to deal with someone who most likely has borderline personality disorder. She ended up indigent and on my couch for far, far too long. When I told her it was time to leave and gave her a month and a half to find a new apartment, she became violently angry, so I had to have the local constabulary escort her off my premises. An abrupt, sad, end to our cordial friendship. Her behaviour pretty much matches what I've read about borderline personality disorder, but as far as I know no one has ever done a formal clinical diagnosis. One of her classic behaviours is to demand that the world dance to her tune, and had a fit if it didn't go her way, in much the same way as a four-year-old. For actual four-year-olds, the world revolves around them, as their parents care for them. In the healthy course of development, one begins to realize that the world, in fact, does not revolve around the subject, the subject gradually fits into larger and larger social groups, and eventually the person must come to terms with the reality that the world is, and the mature person has to adapt to the world, not the other way around.
Yet... in our modern and especially Victimhood culture, individuals are encouraged to do exactly the opposite: to demand that the world conform to their own personal preferences, and a great many get as enraged as a four-year-old when this does not happen. Observe, for example, how the "Trans" people react whenever anyone publicly states the painfully obvious idea that men are men and women are women. (Yes, I know there is a very tiny fraction of the population with psychological issues, and they should not be harmed for that reason, but that becomes the motte-and-bailey fallacy.) Basically "Trans" just *want* the idea that you can just decide to be whatever sex you choose today to be true, and get upset if they don't get their way, just as spoiled children do. Or the way the Professional Managerial Class acts in foreign policy decisions, as you've written about. I want, therefore, the world must give, which is exactly the way a child thinks.
I know the pedagogy of the past 40 years has focused on making sure that every individual's feeling is "respected" and "acknowledged" and, unfortunately, freely expressed, so we have created at least two generations of people who have, in essence, never been taught to behave as functional adults. They are, emotionally, overgrown children.
What do you think that is doing in the psychic planes?

I ask that because, now that the said person who was staying at my home for so long is gone, I cleaned the physical space, which was tough enough, and am calling in some occult friends to do a good cleaning of the psychic miasma that still pervades my space, although it is greatly ameliorated by my daily Sphere of Protection ritual.
If the borderline personality of an individual leaves such a psychic gunk behind, would the inadvertent creation of what is, effectively, a borderline personality culture not also be building up an unhealthy pile of psychic gunk?

Bruce AKA Renaissance Man

replies.title: Re: A Juvenile Culture
replies.content: Of course it is. Just as a major factor in individual karma consists of the thoughts and feelings you encourage and allow, a major factor in collective karma consists of the thoughts and feelings that a culture encourages and allows. comments.title: Q
comments.content:
I'm trying to understand karma and how it relates to consciousness, intent. Is karma about the conscious effect your action has on others vs action you had no control over, or the conscious mores of your culture?

For example, for most of civilizational history, we've ignored children, thinking of them as a fact of procreation, or used them as labour; men rarely were good fathers, there were plenty of bastard children, but in modern day, our conscious understanding of having child, or being a father/mother, is a lot more developed and has a lot more moral content around your roles and responsibilities, as well as law.

How does one distinguish a karmically salient act from a mere moral fad created by civilization to corral behavior a certain way?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
There's collective karma as well as individual karma. Moral fads generate collective karma; your choices generate individual karma. Since karma isn't about moral judgment -- it's simply the rule that every thought, word, and deed has effects that circle around to the person who did them -- it all flows together. The karma you get from just following your culture's prompts is among other things the karma of unthinking obedience; the karma you get from doing something deliberately is among other things the karma of exercising your will.

Complex? You bet. That's why it takes many lives to pass through the human level.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
On an individual level, it represents the union between the lower self and the higher self -- in other terms, between the personality that's developed during this one incarnation and the individuality, the self that remains in and out of incarnation -- in a useful Zen phrase, "the face you had before your mother and father were born."
